name: Pocong
alias: the shroud ghost · the bound dead
body: In Muslim burial the body is wrapped in white cloth, the kain kafan, and tied at the crown of the head, under the chin, and at the feet. The ties are meant to be loosened once the body is in the grave, a small last kindness so the dead may go free. If they are forgotten, the soul cannot leave, and after forty days it rises still bound: a face peering from a knot of grave-cloth, moving the only way a thing tied at the ankles can, in slow, jolting hops.

It is not malicious so much as desperate. The Pocong wants only release, and it returns to those who knew it in life, drawn back along familiar paths to the people who might still help. It asks without words, by its mere appearance at the roadside or the gate, to have the binding at last untied so that it can finish the journey it was meant to take and rest.

What lingers is not fear of harm but the pity underneath it, the sense of a thing caught at the threshold and unable to cross, hopping back to your door night after night because it has nowhere else to go. Along the quiet stretches of road near a fresh burial ground, drivers in Java and across the region still slow at the sight of anything pale and upright in the dark, and the old people say plainly that the kindest thing you can do for such a visitor is not to run, but to loosen the cloth.
note: To free it, the head-tie must be undone; loosen the knot at the crown and chin and the Pocong rests at last, the journey completed. It is most often encountered along quiet roads at night and near fresh graves, in the weeks before the fortieth day has passed. The horror of it lies less in malice than in helplessness, a soul stranded at the threshold by a forgotten gesture.

The figure belongs above all to Indonesian and broader Malay tradition and is a fixture of the region's horror cinema, where its stiff hopping silhouette is instantly recognised. Because the binding ritual it depends on is a real and observed part of Islamic burial, the tale carries a quiet practical reminder to those who prepare the dead: undo the ties, lest the one you bury come hopping home.
